skill.name: backend-patterns
skill.description: Backend architecture patterns, API design, database optimization, and server-side best practices for Node.js, Express, and Next.js API routes.
skill.summary: Practical backend patterns for APIs, data access, authentication, caching, queues, and logging.
skill.author: affaan-m

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported a high score because Markdown code fences and backend sample code matched command, network, environment, and crypto heuristics. Manual review found no executable scripts, no prompt injection attempt, and no evidence of data exfiltration. The remaining concern is low because users may copy simplified examples into production without adding full hardening.

low_findings.title: Markdown Examples Misclassified as Command Execution
low_findings.description: The external command findings point to fenced TypeScript examples and API route samples, not executable skill code. No shell command, Ruby backtick execution, or runtime hook was found in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matched locations are inside Markdown code fences and describe backend patterns. There is no separate script file or instruction that executes these snippets automatically.

low_findings.title: Prototype Manipulation Finding Is Error Subclass Setup
low_findings.description: The prototype manipulation finding is Object.setPrototypeOf used in a custom Error subclass. This is a common TypeScript pattern and does not modify attacker-controlled prototypes.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code sets ApiError.prototype on the current instance after calling super. No user input or global prototype mutation is present.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Access Is a JWT Example
low_findings.description: The environment access finding is a sample that reads JWT_SECRET for token verification. It does not print, transmit, or otherwise expose the secret.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment variable is used only as a local verification secret in documentation. The example is legitimate but simplified for production use.

low_findings.title: Network and Reconnaissance Matches Are Backend Terms
low_findings.description: The network findings match variable names and rate limiter request tracking, not Python HTTP libraries or scanning behavior. The reconnaissance findings are REST routes, database fetch examples, or request metadata.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines implement an in-memory rate limiter example and use a local Map of timestamps. No outbound network call or host discovery behavior is present.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto Findings Are Textual False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography findings do not correspond to MD5, SHA1, DES, or similar algorithms in the reviewed locations. The only crypto-related sample uses crypto.randomUUID for request identifiers.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations contain description text, a heading, and a permission lookup. No weak cryptographic function call appears at those lines.

content.value_statement: Backend services often grow difficult to maintain as routes, database logic, auth, and jobs spread across files. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical patterns for clearer Node.js, Express, and Next.js backends.
actual_capabilities: Explains RESTful API structure with resource routes, filtering, sorting, and pagination.
Shows repository and service layer patterns for separating data access from business logic.
Provides database guidance for column selection, batching, transactions, and cache-aside reads.
Outlines centralized error handling, retry backoff, JWT validation, and role permissions.
Covers rate limiting, background queues, and structured logging for server-side operations.
limitations: Examples are reference patterns and are not a complete production framework.
The skill focuses on TypeScript, Node.js, Express-style APIs, Supabase, Redis, and Next.js routes.
Security examples are simplified and still require project-specific hardening and testing.
It does not inspect a repository unless the user provides files or asks for an implementation review.

use_cases.title: Refactor API Layers
use_cases.description: Separate route handlers, business logic, and database access using repository and service patterns.

use_cases.title: Improve Data Access
use_cases.description: Reduce N+1 queries, add caching, and structure transaction boundaries for database-heavy endpoints.

use_cases.title: Standardize Backend Reviews
use_cases.description: Evaluate authentication, authorization, error handling, rate limiting, queues, and logging against common patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Backend Pattern
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning a new API feature
prompt_templates.prompt: Use backend-patterns to recommend a simple structure for a new API endpoint that reads and updates database records.

prompt_templates.title: Review an Endpoint
prompt_templates.prompt: Use backend-patterns to review this API route for layering, validation, error handling, and data access issues.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Data Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use backend-patterns to design a backend workflow with repository access, transaction handling, caching, and clear failure behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Backend Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Use backend-patterns to audit this backend module for API design, auth boundaries, rate limits, background jobs, logging, and maintainability.

output_examples.input: Review a route that queries users inside a loop.
output_examples.output: The response would identify the N+1 query risk, recommend batching related IDs, and suggest moving data access into a repository method.

output_examples.input: Plan authentication for a protected API route.
output_examples.output: The response would outline token extraction, JWT verification, role checks, consistent errors, and where the handler should receive the authenticated user.

output_examples.input: Improve reliability for a slow third-party operation.
output_examples.output: The response would suggest retry backoff, a background queue, structured logging, and rate limit protection around the user-facing endpoint.

best_practices: Ask for a focused review area, such as auth, caching, database access, or route structure.
Provide representative endpoint and repository files when asking for implementation guidance.
Treat examples as patterns to adapt, then validate them with tests and production security controls.
anti_patterns: Do not copy simplified examples into production without validation, authorization, and error handling.
Do not use in-memory rate limiting as the only control for distributed production systems.
Do not mix route handling, business rules, database queries, and logging in one unstructured function.

faq.question: What frameworks does this skill focus on?
faq.answer: It focuses on TypeScript backend work with Node.js, Express-style APIs, Supabase, Redis, and Next.js API routes.

faq.question: Can it generate complete backend code?
faq.answer: It can guide implementation, but the source skill is a pattern guide rather than a full code generator.

faq.question: Does it cover authentication?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes JWT verification, request authentication, role permissions, and protected handler patterns.

faq.question: Does it cover database performance?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers column selection, batching to avoid N+1 queries, transactions, and Redis caching patterns.

faq.question: Is it safe for production use?
faq.answer: The skill is safe to publish, but its sample patterns need project-specific review before production deployment.

faq.question: Can Claude Code or Codex use it during repository work?
faq.answer: Yes. Claude Code and Codex can apply the patterns when reviewing or editing backend files provided by the user.
